questionText: In 1897, Bal Gangadhar Tilak was tried for sedition by the British Government for exciting disaffection through an article he published in which one of the following?
explanation: Bal Gangadhar Tilak was one of the first persons to be convicted of sedition in colonial India. Tilak faced three trials in cases related to sedition (1897, 1909 and 1916) and was imprisoned twice. In 1897, he was charged with sedition for exciting disaffection through an article he published in his weekly Kesari and was sentenced to 12-month imprisonment. In 1909, he was tried for a second time for sedition for what he had written in 1908 in his Kesari about a bomb thrown by Bengal revolutionaries in Muzzaffarpur, resulting in the death of two innocent European women. He was represented by MA Jinnah, but his application for bail was rejected. He was sent to Mandalay (Burma) jail for six years. It was proposed to prosecute him for sedition for the third time because of speeches delivered by Tilak in Marathi under the auspices of the Historical Research Society at Belgaum and later at Ahmednagar in 1916 on the subject of Home Rule. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: It was agrarian unrest caused by the oppressive practices of Zamindars in the large parts of Eastern Bengal. The zamindars enhanced rents beyond legal limits and prevented the tenants from acquiring occupancy rights under Act X of 1859. The ryots refused to pay the enhanced rents, challenging the zamindars in the courts. This is the most likely description of:
explanation: Pabna Agrarian Leagues: Peasants of the large parts of Eastern Bengal witnessed agrarian unrest caused by oppressive practices of the zamindars. The zamindars resorted to enhanced rents beyond legal limits and prevented the tenants from acquiring occupancy rights under Act X of 1859. The zamindars resorted to forcible evictions, seizure of cattle and crops, and prolonged, costly litigation in courts where the poor peasant found himself at a disadvantage. The league organized a rent strike—the ryots refused to pay the enhanced rents, challenging the zamindars in the courts. Funds were raised by ryots to fight the court cases. The struggles spread throughout Patna and to other districts of East Bengal. The main form of struggle was that of legal resistance; there was very little violence. Many peasants were able to acquire occupancy rights and resist enhanced rents. The government also promised to undertake legislation to protect the tenants from the worst aspects of zamindari oppression. In 1885, the Bengal Tenancy Act was passed. Deccan Riots: The ryots of the Deccan region of western India suffered heavy taxation under the Ryotwari system. Peasants were trapped in a vicious network with the moneylender as the exploiter and the main beneficiary. These moneylenders were mostly outsiders—Marwaris or Gujaratis. The conditions had worsened due to a crash in cotton prices after the end of the American Civil War in 1864, the Government’s decision to raise the land revenue by 50% in 1867, and a succession of bad harvests. In 1874, the growing tension between the moneylenders and the peasants resulted in a social boycott movement organised by the ryots against the “outsider” moneylenders. Eka Movement: It started towards the end of 1921. The peasant discontent resurfaced in some northern districts of the United Provinces—Hardoi, Bahraich, and Sitapur. The issues involved were: high rents: 50 per cent higher than the recorded rates; oppression of thikadars in charge of revenue collection; and practice of share-rents. The meetings of the Eka or the Unity Movement involved a symbolic religious ritual in which the assembled peasants vowed that they would pay only the recorded rent but would pay it on time; not leave when evicted; refuse to do forced labour; give no help to criminals; abide by panchayat decisions. Mappila Revolt: The Mappilas were the Muslim tenants inhabiting the Malabar region where most of the landlords were Hindus. The Mappilas had expressed their resentment against the oppression of the landlords during the nineteenth century also. Their grievances centred around the lack of security of tenure, high rents, renewal fees, and other oppressive exactions. The Mappila movement merged with the Khilafat agitation of 1919-1924. The leaders of the Khilafat-Non-Cooperation Movement like Gandhi, Shaukat Ali, and Maulana Azad addressed Mappila meetings. After the arrest of national leaders, the leadership passed into the hands of the local Mappila leader. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Vernacular Press Act, 1878:
1. It was passed to limit the freedom of the Indian-language (non-English) press.
2. The newspapers Som Prakash and Dacca Prakash were charged under the Act.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Vernacular Press Act was passed in 1878 in British India to limit the freedom of the Indian-language (non-English) press. It was proposed by Lytton, then Viceroy of India against the rising criticisms of the government in face of widespread famine at the time and the Second Anglo-Afghan War (1878–80) which put a considerable strain on the economy. Later, Lord Ripon repealed the entire Vernacular Press Act of 1878 in 1882. So, statement 1 is correct. To avoid the provisions of the Act, the Amrita Bazar Patrika converted from its original Bengali to an English-language newspaper. However, Som Prakash, Dacca Prakash, Halisahar Patrika, Sulabh Samachar, Bharat Mihir, Sadharani, and Bharat Sanskarak, among others, were charged under the provisions of the act. So, statement 2 is correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to press censorship during the Colonial period, consider the following statements:
1. The Censorship of Press Act of 1799 was enacted by Lord Hastings in anticipation of the French invasion of India.
2. Lord Metcalfe repealed the Licensing regulations of the 1823 ordinance and earned the epithet, “liberator of the Indian press”.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Censorship of Press Act of 1799 was enacted by Lord Wellesley, anticipating the French invasion of India. It imposed almost wartime press restrictions including pre-censorship. These restrictions were relaxed under Lord Hastings, who had progressive views, and in 1818, pre-censorship was dispensed with. So, statement 1 is not correct. Lord Metcalfe repealed the Licensing regulations of the 1823 ordinance and earned the epithet, “liberator of the Indian press”. The new Press Act (1835) required a printer/publisher to give a precise account of the premises of a publication and cease functioning if required by a similar declaration. The result of a liberal press policy was the rapid growth of newspapers. So, statement 2 is correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to Rabindranath Tagore, consider the following statements:
1. He is the writer of the National Anthem of both India and Bangladesh.
2. He founded Shantiniketan that later evolved into Viswa Bharti University.
3. He was the first non-European to win a Nobel Prize for literature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Rabindranath Tagore was respectfully called as Gurudev and Bard of Bengal. He was popular for the contribution he has made in the field of art and literature. He was the first non-European, who received the Nobel Prize in the year of 1913 for his collection of poetry named 'Gitanjali'. So, statement 3 is correct. Apart from being a prolific writer, he was also an influential artist and musician. Rabindranath Tagore was such a great poet that he composed the national anthem of India, Bangladesh and even contributed to the national anthem of Sri Lanka, which led him to become a renowned personality all over the world. Rabindranath Tagore was the only one, who penned down the national anthem for more than one country. So, statement 1 is correct. He was an educationist, who wanted to restructure the education system in India and founded Shantiniketan that later became Viswa Bharti University. So, statement 2 is correct. Rabindranath Tagore had written many literatures, songs and poems such as Gitanjali, Bhikharini, Kabuliwala etc. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer. Relevance: The 161st Birth anniversary of Rabindranath Tagore was celebrated on 7th May, 2022.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
1. Lord Chelmsford was the Viceroy when the Civil Disobedience Movement was launched.
2. Lord Willingdon was the Viceroy at the time of the announcement of the Communal Award.
3. Lord Linlithgow was the Viceroy when World War II broke out.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation: Lord Chelmsford (1868-1933) served as Viceroy and Governor General of India from 1916 to 1921. The Civil Disobedience Movement was launched in 1930 when Lord Irwin was the Viceroy. So, statement 1 is not correct. Lord Willingdon 1931-1936 : Second Round Table Conference (1931) and failure of the conference, resumption of Civil Disobedience Movement. Announcement of Communal Award (1932) under which separate communal electorates were set up. So, statement 2 is correct. ‘Fast unto death’ by Gandhi in Yerwada prison, broken after the Poona Pact (1932). Third Round Table Conference (1932). Launch of Individual Civil Disobedience (1933). The Government of India Act of 1935. Establishment of All India Kisan Sabha (1936) and Congress Socialist Party by Acharya Narendra Dev and Jayaprakash Narayan (1934). Lord Linlithgow (1936-1944) First general elections (1936-37); Congress attained absolute majority. Resignation of the Congress ministries after the outbreak of the Second World War (1939). So, statement 3 is correct. Subhash Chandra Bose elected as the president of Congress at the fifty-first session of the Congress (1938). Resignation of Bose in 1939 and formation of the Forward Bloc (1939). Lahore Resolution (March 1940) by the Muslim League, demand for separate state for Muslims. ‘August Offer’ (1940) by the viceroy; its criticism by the Congress and endorsement by the Muslim League. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Which one of the following setup Rajkot College in Kathiawar and the College at Ajmer in order to provide political training for Indian princes?
explanation: Mayo (1822-1872) was the Viceroy and Governor General of India from 1869 to 1872. He established the Rajkot College in Kathiawar and the Mayo College at Ajmer for political training of Indian princes. The Rajkumar College, Rajkot was established in 1868. It is the oldest of the colleges in India founded for the education of the Princely order. Mayo’s tenure was also known for the establishment of the Statistical Survey of India and Department of Agriculture and Commerce. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to Swami Vivekananda, consider the following statements:
1. He subscribed to the philosophy of neo-Vedanta.
2. His literary works include Raja Yoga and Karma Yoga.
3. He gave a new theory of ethics based on intrinsic purity.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: Swami Vivekananda, originally named Narendranath Datta, was an Indian monk who is known for his intellectual contributions to the field of religion. A chief disciple of Ramakrishna, Vivekananda is known to have introduced Hindu philosophies of yoga and vedanta to the western world. He preached ‘neo-Vedanta’, an interpretation of Hinduism through a Western lens, and believed in combining spirituality with material progress. He subscribed to the Vedanta which he considered a fully rational system with a superior approach. His mission was to bridge the gulf between paramartha (service) and vyavahara (behaviour), and between spirituality and day-to-day life. So, statement 1 is correct. Literary works of Swami Vivekananda: Raja Yoga, Jnana Yoga, Meditation and Its Methods, Vedanta: Voice of Freedom, Lectures on Bhagavad Gita, Karma Yoga, My India: the India Eternal. So, statement 2 is correct. He gave a new theory of ethics and a new principle of morality based on the intrinsic purity and oneness of the Atman. According to him, ethics is a code of conduct that helps a person to be a good citizen. He strove to promote peace and human brotherhood on the spiritual foundation of the Vedantic Oneness of existence. So, statement 3 is correct. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer. Relevance: January 12, 2023, marks the 161st birth anniversary of Swami Vivekananda, observed as National Youth Day.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Macaulay’s Minute:
1. It was framed to establish the need for Indian ‘natives’ to receive English education.
2. It settled the dispute in favour of Anglicists as it limited government resources to be devoted solely to the teaching of Western sciences.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: In 1835, Thomas Babington Macaulay delivered his 'Minute on Indian Education’ to establish the need for Indian 'natives' to receive an English education. So, statement 1 is correct. Macaulay's Minute settled the dispute in favour of Anglicists as it limited government resources to be devoted solely to the teaching of Western sciences and literature in English medium in schools and colleges. So, statement 2 is correct. It argued that Indian learning was inferior to European learning which was true as far as physical and social sciences in the contemporary stage were concerned. So, it decided against the expansion of traditional Indian learning. He recommended downward filtration policy where the government would educate only a few Indian population, who would then educate the rest of the population. Thus, it completely neglected mass education. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to Zakir Hussain Committee on Basic Education, consider the following statements:
1. It was set up at the National Educational Conference organised by the Indian National Congress in 1937 at Wardha.
2. The main principle behind this scheme was ‘learning through activity’.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: A committee was formed under the chairmanship of Dr. Zakir Hussain to derive the educational scheme for British India at the National Educational Conference organised by the Indian National Congress in 1937 at Wardha. So, statement 1 is correct. The Committee submitted the first extensive National Basic Education Scheme based on the above resolutions in its report in March 1938, which is known as the Wardha Scheme of Basic Education. It is also called Nai Talim/Basic Education/Buniyadi Talim (Shiksha)/ Buniyadi Shiksha. The main principle behind this scheme was ‘learning through activity’. It focused on various vocational courses and handicraft training. It was based on Gandhi’s ideas published in a series of articles in the weekly Harijan. So, statement 2 is correct. The main features of the Wardha Scheme are as follows: Inclusion of a basic handicraft in the syllabus. First seven years of schooling to be an integral part of a free and compulsory nationwide education system (through mother tongue). Teaching to be in Hindi from class II to VII and in English only after class VIII. Ways to be devised to establish contact with the community around schools through service. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Which one of the following committees recommended free, universal and compulsory elementary education for the 6-11 years age group during colonial India?
explanation: The Sergeant Scheme, formally known as the Report of the Sargent Commission on Post-War Education Development in India, was a 1944 memorandum prepared at the behest of the British-run Government of India that outlined the future development of literacy and education in India. It recommended: pre-primary education for 3-6 years age group; free, universal and compulsory elementary education for 6-11 years age group; high school education for 11-17 years age group for selected children, and a university course of 3 years after higher secondary; high schools to be of two types: (i) academic and (ii) technical and vocational; adequate technical, commercial and arts education; abolition of intermediate course; liquidation of adult illiteracy in 20 years; stress on teachers’ training, physical education, education for the physically and mentally handicapped. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sl. No. Institution Established by
1. Calcutta Madrasah : Richard Wellesley
2. Sanskrit College : Jonathan Duncan
3. Fort William College : Warren Hastings
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation: The Calcutta Madrasah was established by Warren Hastings in 1781 for the study of Muslim law and related subjects. So, pair 1 is not correctly matched. The Sanskrit College was established by Jonathan Duncan at Benaras in 1791 for study of Hindu law and philosophy. So, pair 2 is correctly matched. Fort William College was set up by Richard Wellesley in 1800 for the training of civil servants of the Company in the languages and customs of Indians. The establishment of the college at fort William’s was not favoured by the Court of Directors. Finally, in 1806, the Court of Directors decided to set up a training institution at Haileybury in England which was accorded a statutory status by the Charter Act of 1813. So, pair 3 is not correctly matched. So, only one of the above pairs is correctly matched. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: This police commission was established by Lord Curzon. It recommended the appointment of Indians at the officer level in the police. This is the most likely description of:
explanation: In 1902-03, a Police Commission was established for the Police reforms under Sir Andrew Fraser by Lord Curzon. It recommended the appointment of Indians at the officer level in the police. Indians could rise only to the ranks of Inspector of Police, the senior N.C.O. position. However, they were not part of the Indian Imperial Police. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History
topic: British Administration and Policies in India

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Downward Filtration Theory of education:
1. It was based on the idea to educate a small section of the upper class who would act as interpreters between the government and masses and enrich the vernaculars.
2. Wood’s despatch recommended the continuation of the Downward Filtration Theory.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Downward Filtration Theory: The British planned to educate a small section of upper and middle classes, thus creating a class “Indian in blood and colour but English in tastes, in opinions, in morals and in intellect” who would act as interpreters between the government and masses and would enrich the vernaculars by which knowledge of Western sciences and literature would reach the masses. This was called the ‘downward filtration theory’. So, statement 1 is correct. Modern ideas, if not education, did filter down to the masses, though not in a form desired by the rulers, but through political parties, press, pamphlets, public platforms, etc. Wood’s despatch: In 1854, Charles Wood prepared a despatch on an educational system for India. Considered the “Magna Carta of English Education in India”, this document was the first comprehensive plan for the spread of education in India. It asked the Government of India to assume responsibility for the education of the masses, thus repudiating the ‘downward filtration theory’, at least on paper. So, statement 2 is not correct. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Indian Universities Act, 1904, consider the following statements:
1. It was enacted during the tenure of Lord Linlithgow.
2. It was based on the recommendations of the Raleigh Commission.
3. Under the Act, majority fellows at the universities were to be nominated by the Government.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: George Nathaniel Curzon was a British Conservative statesman who served as Viceroy of India from 1899 to 1905. So, statement 1 is not correct. In the early 20th century, the official view was that under private management, the quality of education had deteriorated and educational institutions acted as factories producing political revolutionaries. Nationalists accepted the decline in quality but accused the Government of not doing anything to eradicate illiteracy. In 1902, the Raleigh Commission was set up to go into the conditions and prospects of universities in India and to suggest measures for improvement in their constitution and working. The commission precluded reporting on primary or secondary education. Based on its recommendations, the Indian Universities Act was passed in 1904. So, statement 2 is correct. As per the Act, universities were to give more attention to study and research; the number of fellows of a university and their period in office was reduced and most fellows were to be nominated by the Government; Government was to have powers to veto universities’ senate regulations and could amend these regulations or pass regulations on its own; conditions were to be made stricter for affiliation of private colleges; and five lakh rupees were to be sanctioned per annum for five years for improvement of higher education and universities. So, statement 3 is correct. Curzon justified greater control over universities in the name of quality and efficiency, but actually sought to restrict education and to discipline the educated towards loyalty to the Government. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Nai Talim:
1. It was based on Gandhi’s ideas published in a series of articles in the weekly Harijan.
2. Its main idea was educating the child through the medium of the productive activity of a suitable handicraft.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The Indian National Congress organised a National Conference on Education in October 1937 in Wardha. In light of the resolutions passed there, the Zakir Hussain committee formulated a detailed national scheme for basic education. It is also called Nai Talim/Basic Education/Buniyadi Talim (Shiksha)/ Buniyadi Shiksha. The main principle behind this scheme was ‘learning through activity. It was based on Gandhi’s ideas published in a series of articles in the weekly Harijan. Gandhi thought that Western education had created a gulf between the educated few and the masses and had also made the educated elite ineffective. So, statement 1 is correct. The scheme provided for the inclusion of basic handicrafts in the syllabus. A suitable technique was to be devised with a view to implementing the main idea of basic education—educating the child through the medium of the productive activity of a suitable handicraft. So, statement 2 is correct. First seven years of schooling to be an integral part of a free and compulsory nationwide education system (through mother tongue). Teaching to be in Hindi from class II to VII and in English only after class VIII. Ways were to be devised to establish contact with the community around schools through service. The system, rather than being a methodology for education, was an expression of an idea for a new life and a new society. The basic premise was that only through such a scheme would India be an independent and non-violent society. This scheme was child-centered and cooperative. There was not much development of this idea, because of the start of the Second World War and the resignation of the Congress ministries (October 1939). Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Development of Judiciary in British India, consider the following statements:
1. District Fauzdari Adalats were established by Lord Wellesly to settle civil disputes.
2. Lord Curzon for the first time brought European subjects under the jurisdiction of local courts.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: District Diwani Adalats and District Fauzdari Adalats were established by Warren Hastingsin districts to try civil disputes and criminal disputesrespectively. District Diwani Adalats were placed under the collector and District Fauzdari Adalats were placed under an Indian officer assisted by qazis and muftis. These adalats also were under the general supervision of the collector. So, statement 1 is not correct. The Cornwallis Code introduced by Cornwallis separated the revenue and justice administration. It also brought European subjects under the jurisdiction of local courts. Government officials were made answerable to the civil courts for actions done in their official capacity. So, statement 2 is not correct. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Aitchison Commission:
1. It was appointed to prepare a scheme of modern education in Western Sciences for Indians.
2. It looked into the question of employment of Indians in the un-covenanted services.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: A Commission headed by Sir Charles Aitchison was appointed in 1886 to prepare a scheme of admission of Indians to every branch of public service. It was expected to look into the question of employment of Indians not only in appointments, ordinarily reserved by law for members of the covenanted civil service but also in the un-covenanted service covering lower-level administrative appointments. So, statement 1 is not correct and 2 is correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History
topic: British Administration and Policies in India

questionText:
With reference to Civil Services in India, consider the following statements:
1. The Lord Macaulay’s Report of the Select Committee of the British Parliament introduced the concept of a merit-based modern Civil Service in India.
2. The Charter Act of 1853 permitted Indians to appear in the civil services exam through a system of open competitive examination.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Civil Servants were used to be nominated by the Directors of the East India Company and thereafter trained at Haileybury College in London and then sent to India. Following Lord Macaulay’s Report of the Select Committee of the British Parliament, the concept of a merit-based modern Civil Service in India was introduced in 1854. So, statement 1 is correct. The Charter Act of 1853 permitted Indians to appear in the civil services exam through a system of open competitive examination. So, statement 2 is correct. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History
topic: British Administration and Policies in India

questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the development of Education under the British rule in India:
1. Under the 1913 Resolution on Education policy, the British government agreed to provide free primary education to all.
2. In 1902, Raleigh Commission was set up to go into conditions and prospects of universities in India.
3. The Sergeant plan of 1944 provided for technical, commercial and arts education.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: In 1906, the progressive state of Baroda introduced compulsory primary education throughout its territories. National leaders urged the government to do so for British India. In its 1913 Resolution on Education Policy, the government refused to take up the responsibility of compulsory education, but accepted the policy of removal of illiteracy and urged provincial governments to take early steps to provide free elementary education to the poorer and more backward sections. Hence, Statement 1 is incorrect. In 1902, Raleigh Commission was set up to go into conditions and prospects of universities in India and to suggest measures for improvement in their constitution and working. Based on its recommendations, the Indian Universities Act was passed in 1904. Hence, Statement 2 is correct. The Sergeant Plan was worked out by the Central Advisory Board of Education in 1944. It recommended adequate technical, commercial and arts education; pre-primary education for 3-6 years age group and free universal and compulsory elementary education for 6-11 years age group; abolition of intermediate course; liquidation of adult illiteracy in 20 years. Hence, Statement 3 is correct. The Indian Universities Act was passed in 1904. As per the Act Universities were to give more attention to study and research. The number of fellows of a university and their period in office were reduced and most fellows were to be nominated by the Government. Government was to have powers to veto universities’ senate regulations and could amend these regulations or pass regulations on its own. Conditions were to be made stricter for affiliation of private colleges; and Five lakh rupees were to be sanctioned per annum for five years for improvement of higher education and universities. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

questionText: Which one of the following leaders started the newspaper called ‘Voice of India’?
explanation: Dadabhai Naoroji: He was born in Bombay on 4 September 1825 in a priestly Parsi family. As a consequence of his outstanding performance at the Elphinstone College, Naoroji obtained the Clare Scholarship. Soon after graduation in 1845, he became the first Indian to be appointed Professor at Elphinstone. On 27 June 1855 he left for London to join the business as a partner in Cama & Co., the first Indian company to be established in London. Four years later he started his own firm Naoroji & Co. Later he became a Professor of Gujarati at University College London. In 1867 he founded the East India Association in London, one of the predecessor organizations of the Indian National Congress with the aim of putting across the Indian point of view before the British public. He was appointed as Justice of the Peace in 1883, started a newspaper called 'Voice of India' and was elected to the Bombay Municipal Corporation for the second time. In August 1885 he joined the Bombay Legislative Council at the invitation of the Governor, Lord Reay. In 1902 he was elected as a Member of the Liberal Party in the House of Commons, representing Central Finsbury, he was the first British Indian MP. He presided over the Indian National Congress Annual Sessions in 1886 (Calcutta), 1893 (Lahore) and 1906 (Calcutta). He was a prominent nationalist of progressive views. He belonged to the school of moderates and was a great believer in constitutional methods. Although he was a champion of Swadeshi, he was not against the use of machines for organizing key industries in the country. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the ‘Dayanand Saraswati,’ consider the following statements:
1. He strongly opposed idol worship and polytheism.
2. He promoted the vedic notion of Chaturvarna system.
3. He published his views in Satyarth Prakash.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: ‘Dayananda Saraswati’ (Mulshankar) (1824–83) was born in Gujarat. He wandered as an ascetic for 15 years in search of truth then established the Arya Samaj unit in Bombay in 1875. He strongly criticized the escapist Hindu belief in Maya (illusion) as the running theme of all physical existence and the aim of human life as a struggle to attain moksha (salvation) through escape from this evil world to seek union with God. He strongly opposed idolatry (idol worship) and launched a frontal attack on Hindu orthodoxy, caste rigidities, untouchability, polytheism, belief in magic, charms and animal sacrifices, the taboo on sea voyages, feeding the dead through shraddhas, etc. So, statement 1 is correct. He subscribed to the Vedic notion of the Chaturvarna system in which a person was identified as a Brahmin, Kshatriya, Vaishya, or Shudra not by birth but according to the occupation and merit of the person. So, statement 2 is correct. His views were published in his famous work, Satyarth Prakash (The True Exposition). He took inspiration from the Vedas and considered them to be ‘India’s Rock of Ages’ and gave the slogan “Back to the Vedas”. So, statement 3 is correct. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer. Relevance: Prime Minister inaugurated the 200th birth anniversary celebrations of Maharishi Dayanand Saraswati.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Pherozeshah Mehta:
1. He was one of the founders of the Bombay Presidency Association and the Indian National Congress.
2. He was elected as a member of the Imperial Legislative Council.
3. He presided over the second session of the Indian National Congress in 1886.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: Bombay Presidency Association was started in 1885 by Pheroshah Mehta, Badruddin Tayabji and KT Telang. It was formed in opposition to the Ilbert Bill and Lytton's other reactionary policies. Mehta was also one of the founders of the Indian National Congress. Hence, born in 1845 in Bombay, Pherozeshah Mehta came under Dadabhai Naoroji's influence while studying law in London during the 1860s. So, statement 1 is correct. He was a member of the Bombay Legislative Council from 1886 and was elected to the governor-general's Imperial Legislative Council in 1893. He was a powerful debater and his speeches were marked by boldness, lucidity, incisiveness, a ready wit, and quick repartee, and ascertain literary quality. For example: In 1901, a Bill was brought in the Bombay Legislative to take away the peasant's right to land ownership to prevent him from bartering it out because of his thriftlessness. Denying this charge and opposing the bill, Mehta defended the peasant's right to have some joy, color, and moments of brightness in his life. So, statement 2 is correct. He presided over the sixth session of the Indian National Congress in 1890. The second session of INC was presided by Dadabhai Naoroji. During a legal defense of a Bombay commissioner, he noted the need for municipal government reforms. He later drew up the Municipal Act of 1872, for which he was called the "father of municipal government in Bombay." He became a commissioner himself in 1873 and served as chairman in 1884–85 and 1905. From about the middle of the 1890s till his death in 1915, he was a dominant figure in the Indian National Congress. He was knighted in 1904. After a trip to England in 1910, Mehta was appointed vice-chancellor of the University of Bombay (now the University of Mumbai). In 1911 he helped found the Central Bank of India, financed and controlled by Indian interests. So, statement 3 is not correct. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Which of the following changes in the judicial system were introduced by Lord Cornwallis in British India?
1. Separation of revenue and justice administration.
2. The principle of sovereignty of law was established.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: The beginning of a common law system can be traced to the establishment of ‘Mayor’s Courts’ in Madras, Bombay, and Calcutta in 1726 by the East India Company. Lord Cornwallis made the following changes: There was a separation of revenue and justice administration. It separated the posts of the Civil judge and the Collector. So, point 1 is correct. European subjects were also brought under jurisdiction. Government officials were answerable to the civil courts for actions done in their official capacity. The principle of sovereignty of law was established. So, point 2 is correct. Under Lord William Bentick, the four Circuit Courts were abolished and their functions transferred to collectors under the supervision of the commissioner of revenue and circuit. He abolished the Provincial Court of Appeal and Circuit. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to Indian artist, Raja Ravi Verma, consider the following statements:
1. He is considered among the first Indian artists to use oil paints.
2. He is credited to open a Lithographic Press in Bombay in 1894.
3. Pictures in popular comic book series ‘Amar Chitra Katha’ are inspired by his work.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: A prolific artist, Raja Ravi Varma (1848-1906), born in Kilimanoor, erstwhile Travancore state of present-day Kerala. He is remembered for giving Indians their western, classical representations of Hindu gods and goddesses. He is well known for his realistic paintings based on religion and mythology. He continues to be regarded as the most important representative of the Europeanised school of painting in India. He worked on both portrait and landscape paintings, and is considered among the first Indian artists to use oil paints. So, statement 1 is correct. Raja Ravi Varma aspired to take his art to the masses and the intent led him to open a Lithographic Press in Bombay in 1894. The first picture printed at Varma’s press was reportedly The Birth of Shakuntala, followed by numerous mythological figures and saints such as Adi Shankaracharya. So, statement 2 is correct. His 1873 painting, Nair Lady Adorning Her Hair, won Varma prestigious awards including Governor’s Gold Medal in the Madras Presidency, and Certificate of Merit at an exhibition in Vienna. The popularity of the prints, in fact, continued till modern times, with Varma’s style acting as inspiration for artists who illustrated the popular comic book series Amar Chitra Katha. Anant Pai, the creator of Amar Chitra Katha comics, asked the comic book artists to look at Ravi Varma's paintings and draw similar pictures. So, statement 3 is correct. In 1904, the British colonial government awarded Varma with the Kaiser-i-Hind Gold Medal. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer. Relevance: Ahead of Raja Ravi Varma’s 175th birth anniversary, there is a proposal to the Central Government urging to posthumously confer the Bharat Ratna on him.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to Indian revolutionary freedom fighter Alluri Sitharama Raju, consider the following statements:
1. He led the Rampa Rebellion against the Madras Forest Act of 1882.
2. He was inspired by the Non-Cooperation Movement and persuaded people to wear khadi and give up drinking.
3. He fought for the cause of Podu cultivation.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Alluri Sitharama Raju is believed to have been born in present-day Andhra Pradesh in 1897 or 1898, and is known for leading guerrilla resistance against the British. The movement that took place in hill blocks of Rampa and Gudem was called the ‘Rampa rebellion’. The Manyam or Rampa Rebellion went on from 1922 to 1924. During this time Raju often fought British troops, and got the title of “Manyam Veerudu” or the “Hero of Jungle”. He began to organise Adivasis against the atrocities by the police, the forest and revenue officials and extensively toured the 'Manyam' area. He told them that they were the sole owners of the forest produce and prepared them to fight against the oppressive Madras Forest Act, 1882. So, statement 1 is correct. Alluri Sitarama Raju’s revolt against the British gained momentum after he opposed the strict implementation of the Madras Forest Act, 1882 that makes the life of tribal people miserable by prohibiting them from Podu cultivation (age-old-cultivation by the tribal where they cannot use the cultivation process of the plains). So, statement 3 is correct. Raju was inspired by the Non-Cooperation Movement and talked about the greatness of Mahatma Gandhi. He persuaded people to wear khadi and give up drinking. But at the same time, he asserted that India could be liberated only by the use of force, not non-violence. So, statement 2 is correct. Anti-government sentiments were brewing among the locals, and this was seen in the looting of police stations in 1922 by tribals under the leadership of Raju. Aided by local support, warfare was carried out in the next couple of years, but Raju was eventually captured by the British and executed in 1924. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer. Knowledge Box Rampa Rebellion: British rule threatened podu cultivation, a type of shifting cultivation where trees and plants are cleared off a patch of land by burning them, and the ashes were mixed with soil as manure to cultivate new crops. This got banned by the government as they secured forest land for their own use, calling it ‘reserved’ forest’. Secondly, the collection of minor forest produce, such as leaves and roots, got banned under the Forest Act of 1882. Tribal people were also forced into labor for the colonial government. At the time, the hill areas in Ganjam, Visakhapatnam, and Godavari were under colonial rule, but local kings acted as muttadars or feudal lords. The British gave a commission to muttadars for extracting rent on their behalf from the people. Relevance: The Prime Minister unveiled a 30-foot-tall bronze statue of Alluri Sitharama Raju, in Andhra Pradesh, to mark his 125th birth anniversary.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sl. No. Name Contribution in freedom struggle
1. Durgawati Devi : Helped Bhagat Singh escape after the Saunders killing
2. Rani Chennamma : Launched the Heraka religious movement
3. Begum Hazrat Mahal : Led rebel soldiers at Lucknow in the revolt of 1857
4. Velu Nachiyar Reclaimed her dominion of Kittur from English
Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
explanation: Durgawati Devi, who was popularly known as Durga Bhabhi, was born in Allahabad in 1907. She was a revolutionary who joined the armed struggle against colonial rule. A member of the Naujawan Bharat Sabha, she helped Bhagat Singh escape in disguise from Lahore after the 1928 killing of British police officer John P Saunders. Later, as revenge for the hanging of Bhagat Singh, Rajguru, and Sukhdev, she made an unsuccessful attempt to kill the former Punjab Governor, Lord Hailey. So, pair 1 is correctly matched. Gaidinliu was at the forefront of the Heraka movement, which was first started by her cousin Haipou Jadonang. Heraka was a socio-religious movement that arose in the 1920s in the Zeliangrong territory. Heraka, which literally means pure, is a monotheistic religion where the followers worshipped Tingkao Ragwang. Rani Chennamma was among the first rulers to lead an armed rebellion against British rule. She fought back against the attempt to control her dominion, Kittur in present-day Karnataka, in 1824 after the death of her young son. So, pair 2 is not correctly matched. Begum Hazrat Mahal took over the reins at Lucknow where the rebellion broke out on June 4, 1857. Her son, Birjis Qadir, was proclaimed the nawab and a regular administration was organized with important offices shared equally by Muslims and Hindus. So, pair 3 is correctly matched. Born in Ramnathpuram in 1730, Velu Nachiyar was the first queen to have ever actively opposed British rule. She fought against the colonial rulers many years before the Sepoy Mutiny. In collaboration with Hyder Ali and Gopala Nayaker, she waged a war against the British and emerged victorious. Eventually, she went on to produce the first human bomb as well as establish the first army of trained women soldiers in the late 1700s. So, pair 4 is not correctly matched. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer. Knowledge Box: Rani Gaidinliu was born in 1915 in present-day Manipur. She was a Naga spiritual and political leader who fought the British. She joined the Heraka religious movement which later became a movement to drive out the British. She rebelled against the Empire, and refused to pay taxes, asking people to do the same. The British launched a manhunt, but she evaded arrest, moving from village to village. She was finally arrested in 1932 when she was just 16, and later sentenced for life. She was released in 1947. Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru described Gaidinliu as the “daughter of the hills”, and gave her the title of ‘Rani’ for her courage.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the ‘Total Revolution’ initiated by Jayaprakash Narayan:
1. It was started during the Quit India Movement in 1942.
2. According to him, Total Revolution is a means to achieve the goal of Sarvodaya.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: ‘Total Revolution’ initiated by Jayaprakash Narayan aimed at bringing about a revolutionary change in all aspects of the life of both society and individuals. The objective of this movement was not merely to change the Government, but also to change society and the individual. Total Revolution, as a concept, was put forward by him in the wake of the Bihar Movement in Patna on June 5, 1974. In a public meeting at Gandhi Maidan, he declared that the struggle was not going to be limited to securing the demands of the students, including the resignation of the Minister and the dissolution of the Assembly in Bihar, but would aim at bringing about a Total Revolution or Sampoorna Kranti, which alone could solve the urgent problems of the country and usher in a new society. The Bihar Movement did not aim merely to bring about a few changes here and there; it aimed at a "Total Revolution” in the country. So, statement 1 is not correct. Jayaprakash Narayan (JP) maintained on one occasion: “There is hardly any difference between Sarvodaya and Total Revolution. If there is any, then Sarvodaya is the goal and Total Revolution the means. Total Revolution is a basic change in all aspects of life. There cannot be Sarvodaya without this.” To be precise, Total Revolution is a further extension of Gandhi’s thought on socio-economic problems and technique of change in the context of modern social reality. Rampant corruption in the Indian polity and economic life goaded JP to call for a mass movement. He appealed to the youth to revolt against the existing system and to be the harbingers of change. In spite of being termed as a failure, the mass movement he generated brought about a massive positive change in the systemic structure. So, statement 2 is correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer. Relevance: On the occasion of 75th Independence Day, the Prime Minister of India paid tribute to women freedom fighters for showing the world the true meaning of India’s “Nari shakti”.

questionText: He qualified for the Indian Civil Services exam with a 4th rank in 1920 but resigned later due to involvement in political activities across Bengal. His political guru was Deshbandhu Chitranjan Das. He wrote the books “The Indian Struggle” and “An Indian Pilgrim”. His birthday is celebrated as ‘Parakram Diwas’ every year. This is the most likely description of:
explanation: SC Bose was born on January 23, 1897, in Cuttack, Orissa. (The Government of India had decided to celebrate the birthday of Netaji Subhash Chandra Bose, on 23rd January, as 'Parakram Diwas' every year.) In 1920, he appeared for the Indian Civil Service competitive examination in England and came out fourth in order of merit. However, he was deeply disturbed by the Jallianwalla Bagh massacre and left his Civil Services apprenticeship midway to return to India in 1921. After returning to India, he came under the influence of Mahatma Gandhi and joined the Indian National Congress. On Gandhiji's instructions, he started working under Deshbandhu Chittaranjan Das, who became his political guru. He was also elected President of the Haripura Congress Session in 1938. He was re-elected at the next session at Tripuri (1939). However, his rigid stand on the Indian response to World war 2 was opposed, and he resigned from the post of president. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer

questionText: In the context of India’s colonial history, ‘‘The Fifth Report’’ of 1813 is related to:
options: a) Appointment of Indians in the government regulated University
b) The power to the Indian judges to try europeans
c) Devaluation of the Indian rupee
d) The administration activities undertaken by the East India Company
explanation: In the eighteenth century, many political groups argued that the conquest of Bengal was benefiting only the East India Company but not the British nation as a whole. The British Parliament passed a series of Acts to regulate and control Company rule in India. It forced the Company to produce regular reports on the administration of India and appointed committees to enquire into the affairs of the Company. The Fifth Report was one such report produced by a Select Committee. It became the basis of intense parliamentary debates on the nature of the East India Company’s rule in India. It was submitted to the British Parliament in 1813. The Fifth Report is a part of the series of reports on the administration and activities of the East India Company in India. It reproduced petitions of zamindars and ryots, reports of collectors from different districts, statistical tables on revenue returns, and notes on the revenue and judicial administration of Bengal and Madras (present-day Tamil Nadu) written by officials. Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History
topic: British Administration and Policies in India

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sl.No. Author Book
1. Bankimchandra Chattopadhyaya : Bangadarshan
2. Romesh Chandra Dutt : Samaj
3. Vishnu Shastri Chiplunkar : Nibandhamala
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
explanation: Bankimchandra Chattopadhyaya brought out a journal - the Bangadarshan. It aimed to educate and inspire as many of his countrymen as possible, often written in a humorous and sarcastic style. So, pair 1 is correctly matched. Romesh Chandra Dutt wrote the Samaj in 1893. He idealised the ancient Indian past in his novel where he revealed a conception of Indian nationalism in which Hindus were seen as the key figures. So, pair 2 is correctly matched. Vishnu Shastri Chiplunkar (1850-132) commented on British rule through his book Nibandhmala. It talked about the way English-educated Indians had been affected by English poetry. Chiplunkar himself subscribed to the divine theory and enumerated the advantages that India was deriving as a result of the British colonial connection. So, pair 3 is correctly matched. So, all three of the above pairs are correctly matched. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the developments in education during the Swadeshi Movement of 1905, consider the following statements:
1. Bankim Chandra Chatterjee wrote Sudesha Geetham to arouse nationalistic feelings among people during this time.
2. Rabindranath Tagore composed Amar Sonar Bangla which was adopted by it as its national anthem.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: During the Swadeshi Movement of 1905, the nationalists of all hues took inspiration from songs written by Rabindranath Tagore, Rajnikant Sen, Dwijendralal Ray, Mukunda Das, Syed Abu Mohammad and others. In Tamil Nadu, Subramania Bharathi wrote Sudesha Geetham in 1908. Bharathi was essentially a lyrical poet. “Kannan Pattu” “Nilavum Vanminum Katrum” “Panchali Sabatam” “Kuyil Pattu” are examples of Bharathi’s great poetic output. His writings helped to arouse nationalistic feelings among people. So, statement 1 is not correct. Rabindranath Tagore composed the famous song Amar Sonar Bangla in Bengali language. The song was written in 1905 during the partition of Bengal. This division of Bengal was made along communal lines to undermine India's national movement. This song was meant to rekindle the unified spirit of Bengal, to raise public consciousness against the communal political divide. Later, it inspired the liberation struggle of Bangladesh and was adopted by it as its national anthem. So, statement 2 is correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the development of education during British rule, consider the following statements:
1. Raja Ram Mohan Roy favoured the Government’s proposal of establishment of more oriental colleges in Bengal.
2. Wood’s Dispatch declared that, for higher education, English was the most perfect medium of education.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy protested against the Government’s proposal to strengthen the Calcutta, Madras and Banaras Sanskrit College and establishment of more oriental colleges in Bengal. He wrote to Lord Amherst in 1823 that Sanskrit education could only be expected to load the minds of youth with grammatical niceties and metaphysical distinctions of life, which are of little or no practical use to their possessors or to society. So, statement 1 is not correct. Wood’s Dispatch (1854) declared that, for higher education, English was the most perfect medium of education. It also emphasised the importance of the vernacular languages, it was through the medium of vernacular language that European knowledge could infilter to the masses. It proposed the setting up of vernacular primary schools in the villages at the lowest stage, followed by Anglo-vernacular high schools and affiliated colleges at the district level. So, statement 2 is correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct with reference to Bhagat Singh?
1. He established the Naujawan Bharat Sabha.
2. He was tried in the Lahore Conspiracy case.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation: Bhagat Singh was born in a Sikh family in Banga village of Faisalabad district, now in Pakistan's Punjab province, on 27 September 1907. As his family was deeply inspired by nationalism, he too became actively involved in the country's freedom movements. In 1926, Bhagat Singh established the 'Naujawan Bharat Sabha (Youth Society of India) and joined the Hindustan Republican Association (later known as the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association). So, statement 1 is correct. During that time, he met many anti-colonial activists. In December 1928, Bhagat Singh, along with Sukhdev and Rajguru, planned to avenge the death of Indian nationalist leader Lala Lajpat Rai and plotted to assassinate the Superintendent of Police James Scott in Lahore. However, in a case of mistaken identity, John Saunders, the Assistant Superintendent of Police was shot. Later life of Bhagat Singh: Bhagat Singh had already, before his arrest in 1929, abandoned his belief in terrorism and individual heroic action. He had turned to Marxism and had come to believe that popular broad-based mass movements alone could lead to a successful revolution; in other words, a revolution could only be achieved ‘by the masses for the masses. Bhagat Singh, Rajguru and Sukhdev were hanged by the British on March 23, 1931, in the Lahore Conspiracy case. So, statement 2 is correct. The Philosophy of the Bomb was written by Bhagwaticharan Vohra. ; ’Why i am an Atheist‘ was written by Bhagat Singh. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With regard to the contribution made by Rammanohar Lohia, consider the following statements:
1. He advocated for the adoption of Hindi as India’s national language.
2. He founded the Kisan Mazdoor Praja Party outside the Indian national congress.
3. He was the editor of the journal “Mankind”.
How many of the statements given above are incorrect?
explanation: Statement 1 is correct: Rammanohar lohia advocated for various socio-political reforms in his capacity as party leader, including the abolition of the caste system, the adoption of Hindi as India’s national language, and stronger protection of civil liberties. In 1963 he was elected to the Lok Sabha (the lower house of parliament). Statement 2 is incorrect: J.B. Kripalani founded the Kisan Mazdoor Praja Party (KMPP) in June 1951. The KMPP was a party of Indian National Congress dissidents. In September 1952, the KMPP merged with the Socialist Party to form the Praja Socialist Party. Ram Manohar Lohia was one of the founders of the Congress Socialist Party. From 1934 Lohia became actively involved in the Congress Socialist Party (CSP), founded that year as a left-wing group within the Indian National Congress. He served on the CSP executive committee and edited its weekly journal. Statement 3 is correct: Rammanohar Lohia became a member of the Praja Socialist Party upon its formation in 1952 and served as general secretary for a brief period, but internecine conflicts led to his resignation in 1955. Later that year Lohia established a new Socialist Party, for which he became chairman as well as the editor of its journal, Mankind. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

questionText:
In the context of evolution of Civil Services during the colonial rule in India, consider the following statements:
1. The Civil Services in British India were classified as covenanted and uncovenanted services on the basis of the nature of work, pay scales and appointing authority.
2. The Butler Commission recommended the division of the services into three groups Imperial, Provincial and Subordinate.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Civil Servants for the East India Company used to be nominated by the Directors of the Company and thereafter trained at Haileybury College in London and then sent to India. Following Lord Macaulay’s Report of the Select Committee of British Parliament, the concept of a merit-based modern Civil Service in India was introduced in 1854. The Civil Services in British India were classified as covenanted and uncovenanted services on the basis of the nature of work, pay scales and appointing authority. In 1887, the Aitchison Commission recommended the reorganisation of the services on a new pattern and divided the services into three groups Imperial, Provincial and Subordinate. The Butler Committee was established in 1927 to investigate the relationship between the British Raj and the rulers of Princely States. The committee was chaired by Sir Harcourt Butler and included two other members, William Searle Holdsworth and Sidney Peel. The committee visited 16 states and submitted its report in 1929. So, statement 1 is correct and 2 is not correct. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History
topic: British Administration and Policies in India

questionText:
Consider the following:
1. The Discovery of India
2. The Life Divine
3. Hind Swaraj
4. Truth is God
How many of the above books/journals were written/started by Sri Aurobindo Ghosh?
explanation: Born in 1872, Sri Aurobindo was sent to England at the age of seven, where he did his schooling and went on to a brilliant academic career at Kings College, Cambridge, before returning to India with Maharaja Sayajirao Gaekwad of Baroda in 1893. Sri Aurobindo returned as a strong nationalist and almost immediately began writing on the political situation. The Life Divine: It first appeared serially in the monthly review ‘Arya’ between August 1914 and January 1919. Each installment was written immediately before its publication. In 1939 and 1940. Sri Aurobindo revised The Life Divine for book publication. So, point 2 is correct. So, point 2 is correct. While the discovery of India was written by Jawaharlal Nehru, the books Hind Swaraj and Truth is God were written by Mahatma Gandhi. So, points 1, 3 and 4 are not correct. So, only one of the above books was written by Sri Aurobindo Ghosh. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sl. No. Personality Publication
1. Raja Ram Mohan Roy : Tuhfat-Ul-Muwahhiddin
2. M. G. Ranade Mooknayak
3. Gopal Ganesh Agarkar Sudharak
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation: The Tuhfat-Ul-Muwahhiddin is a short treatise written in 1803 by Raja Ram Mohan Roy (1772-1833), an early colonial Bengal social, educational and religious reformer Written in Persian with an Arabic introduction, the Tuḥfat was the first work of Ram Mohan Roy. The text is a direct attack on the various ills that the author believed plagued Hinduism and Indian society in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. So, pair 1 is correctly matched. Mooknayak: It was started by B.R. Ambedkar. Shahu Maharaj of Kolhapur financed this newspaper. It reflected the conspicuous shift in the socio-political discourse on caste and untouchability in India. Ambedkar’s newspapers, beginning with Mooknayak, a Marathi fortnightly, helped to inaugurate new politics of assertion that challenged the dominant social diseases. So, pair 2 is not correctly matched. Gopal Ganesh Agarkar was an educationist, freedom fighter, and social reformer. He was born on 14 July 1856, in Satara district (present-day Sangli) in Maharashtra. Agarkar established the New English School in Pune along with Tilak and Vishnu Chiplunkar. In 1884, Agarkar and Tilak established the Deccan Education Society in Pune. Agarkar was also associated with the nationalist newspaper Kesari, first published in 1880. He started his own newspaper Sudharak (The Reformer), which challenged both untouchability and casteism. So, pair 3 is correctly matched. So, only two of the above pairs are correctly matched. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following events:
1. Widow Remarriage Act, 1856 was passed
2. Telegraph line was established between Calcutta and Agra
3. First railway line between Bombay and Thane was opened
4. Second Anglo-Sikh War
How many of the above occurred during the viceroyship of Lord Dalhousie (1848 - 1856)?
explanation: Lord Dalhousie (Real name James Andrew Ramsay and he served as a Governor-General from 1848 to 1856): During this period, Second Anglo-Sikh War (1849) was fought in which the Sikhs were defeated again and Dalhousie successfully annexed the whole of Punjab to the British administration. He annexed many states by the doctrine of lapse. During his tenure, the first railway line between Bombay and Thane was opened in 1853 and in the same year, Calcutta and Agra were connected by telegraph. His other reforms include the setting up of P.W.D. and the passing of the Widow Remarriage Act (1856) occured during the tenure of Lord Canning, it was drafted under the tenure of Lord Dalhousie. So, points 2, 3 and 4 are correct. So, only three of the above events occurred during the viceroyship of Lord Dalhousie (1848 - 1856). Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: The ‘Stripurushtulna’ (a comparison between women and men) that criticizes the social differences between men and women, was written by:
explanation: Tarabai Shinde (1850–1910) was a feminist activist who protested patriarchy and caste in 19th century India. She is known for her published work, Stri Purush Tulana ("A Comparison Between Women and Men"), originally published in Marathi in 1882. The pamphlet is a critique of upper-caste patriarchy, and is often considered the first modern Indian feminist text. It was very controversial for its time in challenging the Hindu religious scriptures themselves as a source of women's oppression, a view that continues to be controversial and debated today. Shinde was an associate of social activists Jotirao and Savitribai Phule and was a member of their Satyashodak Samaj ("Truth Finding Community") organisation. The Phules had started the first school for Untouchable caste girls in 1848, as well as a shelter for upper-caste widows in 1854 (who were forbidden from remarrying), and shared with Shinde an awareness of the separate axes of oppression that constitute gender and caste, as well as the intermeshed nature of the two. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with respect to the Indian National Army (INA) trials:
1. Congress adopted a resolution in the Bombay session in support of the Indian National Army’s cause.
2. Prem Kumar Sehgal, Shah Nawaz khan and Gurbaksh Singh Dhillon were the first INA prisoners to face the trial.
3. Following the public protest, the British reduced the death sentence of INA prisoners to deportation for life.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: The Indian National Army trials also known as the INA trials and the Red Fort trials was the British Indian trial of a number of officers of the Indian National Army (INA) between 1945 and 1946, on various charges of treason, torture, murder and abetment to murder. The INA prisoners were the ones who fought with Japanese troops during World War II in Burma. At the first post-War Congress session in September 1945 at Bombay, a strong resolution was adopted declaring Congress support for the INA cause. Those who supported the INA cause in varying degrees, apart from the Congress, included the Muslim League, Communist Party, Unionists, Akalis, Justice Party, Ahrars in Rawalpindi, RashtriyaSwayamsevakSangh, Hindu Mahasabha and the Sikh League. Hence, Statement 1 is correct. British held the first trial at the Red Fort in Delhi in November 1945 and put on dock together a Hindu, Prem Kumar Sehgal, a Muslim, Shah Nawaz Khan, and a Sikh, Gurbaksh Singh Dhillon. These three prisoners were the first to face the trials. Hence, statement 2 is correct. The British never sentenced any prisoners to death. The actual punishment for Shah Nawaz Khan, Gurubaksh Singh Dhillon and PremSahgal were to be dismissed from service and handed transportation for life. However, this punishment too was remitted later. Hence, Statement 3 is incorrect. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Consider the following pairs regarding the Acts on development of Indian Press:
Sr No. Acts Consequences
1. Registration Act, 1867 Every newspaper should print the names of the printers.
2. Licensing Act, 1857 Copy of the published book/newspaper to be submitted to the local government
3. Press Act of 1835 Required a printer/publisher to give a precise account of premises of a publication
How many pairs given above is/are correct?
explanation: Registration Act, 1867 replaced the Metcalfe’s Act of 1835 and was of a regulatory, not restrictive, nature. As per the Act, every book/ newspaper was required to print the name of the printer and the publisher and the place of the publication; and a copy was to be submitted to the local government within one month of the publication of a book. Hence, Pair 1 is correctly matched. Licensing Act, 1857 did not replace Metcalfe’s Act of 1835. Registration Act, 1867 replaced the Metcalfe’s Act of 1835. Due to the emergency caused by the 1857 revolt, Licensing Act, 1857 was enacted. It imposed licensing restrictions in addition to the already existing registration procedure laid down by Metcalfe Act and the government reserved the right to stop publication and circulation of any book, newspaper or printed matter. Hence, Pair 2 is not correctly matched. Metcalfe (governor-general—1835-36) repealed the obnoxious 1823 ordinance and earned the epithet, “liberator of the Indian press”. The new Press Act (1835) required a printer/publisher to give a precise account of premises of a publication and cease functioning, if required by a similar declaration. The result of a liberal press policy was a rapid growth of newspapers. Hence, pair 3 is correctly matched. Therefore, Option (b) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to evolution of Civil services in India:
1. Entry into Imperial Police was thrown open to Indians after 1920.
2. The civil services exam was held only in India after the setting up of the Federal Public Service Commission.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation: Prior to independence superior police officers belonged to the Indian (Imperial) Police appointed by the Secretary of State by competitive examination. Entry into Imperial Police was thrown open to Indians only after 1920 and the following year examinations for the service were conducted both in England and India. So, statement 1 is correct. From 1922 onwards, after the First World War and the Montagu-Chelmsford reforms (Government of India Act, 1919), the Indian Civil Service Examination began to be held in India. It was first conducted in Allahabad and later in Delhi with the setting up of the Federal Public Service Commission. The examination in London continued to be conducted by the Civil Service Commission. So, statement 2 is not correct. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History
topic: British Administration and Policies in India

questionText:
Consider the following statements with respect to Gandhi’s attitude towards actions of Indian National Army (INA):
1. Gandhiji condemned Indian National Army’s action of taking help from Nazi Germany, to liberate India from British rule.
2. Gandhi praised the Indian National Army for its secular nature.
3. Gandhi supported the cause of freedom of the Indian National Army’s prisoners from the British.
How many of the statements given above are correct?
explanation: Gandhi did not condemn the Indian National Army’s action of seeking help from Nazi Germany. His conversation with American Journalist Louis Fischer shows Gandhi’s contempt for British rule in India. Gandhiji observed “There are powerful elements of Fascism in British rule”. So, according to him there was nothing wrong in taking the help of Nazi Germany in the fight against British rule. Hence, Statement 1 is incorrect. Gandhi has a difference of opinion with the violent way of working of Indian National Army. Despite this he praised the INA for its secular nature and bringing together the Indian soldiers without discriminating on the basis of religion or caste. He also admired the selfless sacrifice of INA soldiers and supported the cause of their freedom from the British during the Red Fort Trials. Hence, Statement 2 and Statement 3 are correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

questionText:
It was an important committee formed to address issues regarding the implementation of the Wood’s Despatch of 1854, evaluate the status of elementary education in British territories, and propose ways to enhance it. One of its key recommendations was to grant district and city boards control over primary education. It also recommended that special care was supposed to be taken in advancing the education of girls and women.
Which of the following Commissions are described in the paragraph above?
explanation: Sir William Hunter was an Indian Civil Service officer and a member of the Executive Council of the Viceroy. Lord Ripon appointed on February 3, 1882 the Indian Education Commission under the chairmanship of William Hunter, a member of the executive council of viceroy. Subsequently Indian Universities Act of 1904 and Lord Curzon’s Educational Policy also came into existence to improve upon the educational system of India. The Hunter Education Commission was a body whose goals were to investigate concerns about the Wood’s Despatch of 1854 not being implemented, assess the current state of elementary education in the British territories, and provide recommendations for how to expand and improve it. Sir William Wilson Hunter is in charge. In 1882, he submitted the report. Major recommendations by the Hunter Commission of 1882 resulted in the following changes in the education system of British India: Preference was given to literate candidates for government jobs in the lower levels, along with expansion of primary schools in backward districts. District and municipal boards were entrusted with the management of primary education under the Local Self Government Act. The funds were separated for rural and urban areas to avoid funds earmarked for rural schools being misappropriated by urban schools. Secondary schools were to be established by private parties with funds provided by the government. Model schools fully run by the government were to be opened in each district to guide such private schools. Secondary school curriculum was also revised with academic and vocational courses diversified into different branches. Missionary schools were discouraged, and Indian participation in the private school system was solicited by the raj. Special care was supposed to be taken in advancing the education of girls and women. Therefore, option c is the correct answer.

questionText:
This leader was selected as Imperial Civil Services (ICS) officer in 1869 but he was barred from services in 1869 due to an age dispute, and he was fired in 1871 due to racial discrimination. Later, he led the Indian National Conference session for two years between 1883 and 1885. He also became a senior leader of Indian National Congress.
In the above passage, which among the following personalities has been discussed?
explanation: Surendranath Banerjee was a resident of Calcutta and was born on November 10, 1848. He was known as one of the early political leaders of India during the rule of the Britishers. He was the founding member of the Indian National Association, a nationalist organization. He and Ananda Mohan Bose led the Indian National Conference session for two years between 1883 and 1885. He graduated from the University of Calcutta and, in 1868, traveled to England to compete in an examination for the Indian Civil Service. In 1869 he was barred from services due to an age dispute, and he was fired in 1871 due to racial discrimination. Hence, Option (a) is the correct answer. Satyendra Sinha was a lawyer. He was appointed the first Governor of Bihar and Odisha in 1920 (29th December). He was the first Indian to become a member of the Viceroy's Executive Council. Pherozeshah Mehta was dubbed "Lion of Bombay." He was elected president of the Indian National Congress in 1890. He founded the Bombay Chronicle, an English weekly newspaper, in 1910. For his legal services, he was knighted by the British. W C Banerjee was born on 29th December 1844 in Calcutta. In 1862, he joined a law firm in Calcutta as a clerk from where he got acquainted with law. He later went to England to study law. He was the first President of the Indian National Congress (INC). Womesh Chandra Bonnerjee passed away on 21st July 1906. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the book ‘The History of British India’:
1. It was published by James Mill.
2. It glorified the cultural, economic, and social richness of India.
3. It criticized the exploitative nature of the commercial monopoly of East India Company.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation: The History of British India was published by James Mill in 1817 and soon became the standard reference book on its subject among its British imperialists. So, statement 1 is correct. The History of British India purports to be a study of India in which James set out to attack India's history, character, religion, literature, arts, and laws, also making claims about the influence of the Indian climate. He argued for India's improvement through state-led reforms. Under his influence, a Law Commission was appointed in 1833 that drew up the Indian Penal Code, of 1835. So, statement 2 is not correct. It supported the view of the western world that India should be governed according to civilized European standards. It also opposed the continuance of the Company's trading monopoly and argued that these monopolistic privileges were justifiable if the Company was first established. So, statement 3 is correct. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.

questionText: He was the founder of Newspapers like Prabuddh Bharat and Janata. The papers strongly criticized Congress and right-wing Hindu nationalists for their indifference to the question of caste. Many of his newspapers faced lack of funds due to his firm position on the issue of advertisement. This is the most likely the description of:
explanation: Dr. B. R. Ambedkar: Ambedkar entered the world of newspapers in 1920. In 1920, he launched his first newspaper, Mooknayak. Later, he went on to found three more newspapers - Bahishkrut Bharat, Janata, and Prabuddh Bharat. The papers criticised the Congress and right-wing Hindu nationalists for their indifference to caste issues. Many of his newspapers were cursed by a lack of funds due to his firm position on the issue of advertisement. This is why Mooknayak and Bahishkrut Bharat were eventually closed down. He criticized newspapers in one of his editorials for encouraging irresponsible advertisements that upheld and perpetuated inequality and ritualism. 'Prabuddha Bharata' is a monthly journal of the Ramakrishna Order which was started by Swami Vivekananda. It has been in publication since 1896 and carries articles on social sciences and humanities comprising historical, psychological, cultural, and social sciences themes. ‘Prabuddh Bharat’ is a newspaper Founded by Ambedkar in 1956. As he moved towards Buddhism, he decided to change the name of Janata to Prabuddh Bharat, aligning with his vision of moving “towards an enlightened India ''. Therefore option (d) is the correct answer.

questionText: With reference to the colonial history of India, ‘Goyendas’ were:
explanation: Goyendas were the spies who helped the police. In 1808, Mayo appointed a Superintendent of Police for each division who were helped by a number of spies (goyendas) but these spies committed depredations on local people. Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.

questionText:
With reference to the evolution of the Police system in British India, consider the following statements:
1. The system of putting a division under a Superintendent of Police was introduced by Lord Mayo.
2. An All-India Police Service was set up by the Police Act of 1861.
3. Lord Bentick put the police forces under the charge of the District Collector.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation: The post of the Superintendent of Police (SP) was introduced by Lord Cornwallis, not Lord Mayo, as a part of his famous administrative reforms to the civil services in British India. Hence, statement 1 is incorrect. The Indian Police Act, 1861 (result of recommendations of the Police Reform Commission of 1860), created guidelines for setting up of Provincial Police Services only (not an All-India Police Services). Hence, statement 2 is incorrect. All the Provinces enacted this Act uniformly, which is why the Police system throughout British India was uniform. However, in reality the Police system existed in each Province separately and there was no pan-India police service at that time. The police system in each Province was led by the Inspector General. Lord Bentick, the Governor General of British India from 1828-35 reversed Cornwallis’ policy of Separation of Powers and put the police forces under the charge of the District Collector. Hence, statement 3 is correct. He abolished the office of the Superintendent of Police (SP). The collector was now to head the police force in his jurisdiction and the commissioner in each division was to act as the Superintendent of Police (SP). This arrangement resulted in a badly organised police force, putting a heavy burden on the collector/magistrate. Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
